title: Chapter Three: The Perfect Stranger
content: I didn't sleep.

That's not quite accurate. I lay in my bed from midnight to dawn, which is a kind of sleeping if you count the way time passes without your permission. But my eyes were open the whole while, and my hands wouldn't stop doing this thing — pressing together, pulling apart, pressing again. Like I was checking whether the warmth Maren had left in them was still there.
It was. It is.

The gift hums under my skin like a second pulse, low and constant, inhabiting the space between tendon and bone. I keep expecting it to fade, the way a sound fades when you walk away from its source. But it doesn't fade. If anything, it's louder in the quiet of my room, thrumming along the architecture of my palms as though something in me is listening.

By six I gave up on the pretense of rest. I made tea — steeped it too long, as I always do when I'm tired, then overcorrected with honey until the cup was a viscous, bittersweet thing that coated my tongue and refused to leave. I drank it anyway. Better to taste something wrong than nothing at all.

The journal sat on the kitchen table where I'd left it. I opened it again. The lavender scent hit like a fist to the stomach — or lower, somewhere in the gut where nausea lives — and I had to close it before I'd read a single word. The pages were the same: my grandmother's precise hand, the pressed flowers brown and brittle between the leaves, the ink faded to the color of old bruises. But the smell had changed overnight, or I had. It wasn't soothing anymore. It was desperate. Like the journal itself was trying to tell me something I wasn't ready to hear.
I put it in a drawer. Closed it. Opened it. Closed it again.
Went to work.

Grimm & Root Antiques sits on Cormorant Street between a bookshop that never has customers and a taxidermist that always does. The building is two centuries old and leans slightly to the left, as though it's trying to eavesdrop on the building next door. Mrs. Thorne has owned it for forty-one years and moves through its cluttered aisles with the serene purposefulness of a woman who knows exactly where everything is and refuses to tell anyone else.

The shop bell announced me at seven forty-five. It has a particular sound — not cheerful, not mournful, just *present*, the way a heartbeat is present. You don't notice it until it stops. Mrs. Thorne looked up from the register, assessing me with the quick, cataloging glance she uses for damaged goods.
"You look like something the cat dragged in and then felt guilty about," she said.
"Good morning to you too."
"Tea's in the back. The decent kind, not that honey-swill you make at home."
I didn't ask how she knew about the honey. Mrs. Thorne knows things the way foundations know where the house stands. You don't question it. You just accept that the building isn't going anywhere.

The morning passed in the small rituals of the shop: dusting the cranberry glass, re-tying the price tags that never stay tied, listening to the clock above the mantle tick its steady, indifferent count. My heartbeat kept trying to sync with it and failing, settling into an arrhythmic stutter that made me feel like two people occupying the same chest. One of them was calm. The other one was waiting for something she couldn't name.
At ten seventeen, the bell rang again.
The air changed.

I want to be precise about this, because it matters. The air in Grimm & Root is always a particular thing — dusty, aged, faintly sweet with the residue of a thousand old objects breathing their slow chemical breath. But when the door opened that morning, the temperature didn't shift so much as the weight of it did. The atmosphere grew heavier, thicker, sweet in a different register. Like honey, yes, but cut with something sharper. Turpentine. The smell of someone's workspace, someone who made things.
I looked up.

He stood in the doorway, backlit by the grey morning, and for a moment he was just a silhouette — tall, broad through the shoulder, dark-haired. Then he stepped inside and the light rearranged itself around him, and I could see that he was handsome in a way that felt almost aggressive. Not the soft, apologetic handsomeness of men who are surprised by their own faces. This was a man who knew exactly what he looked like and had decided it wasn't the most interesting thing about him.

The most interesting thing about him, it turned out, was the way he looked at the room. He took it all in — the brass instruments, the steamer trunks, the row of porcelain dolls with their glass eyes and painted smiles — and his expression wasn't the usual half-bored browse of a casual shopper. He was reading it. The way I read objects. The way an archeologist reads soil.

"Good morning," he said, and his voice was the same temperature as the air he'd brought in — warm, weighted, dangerous in the way that only pleasant things can be. "I'm looking for provincial art. Local pieces. Anything that might capture the character of the area."
Mrs. Thorne emerged from behind a display of tarnished silverware. "We have some watercolors in the back. Seascapes, mostly."
"Wonderful." He smiled at her, and I watched Mrs. Thorne — who has not been charmed by anyone in at least two decades — actually smile back. "I'm new to town. Julian Cross. I'm a painter."
"A painter," Mrs. Thorne repeated, as though the profession were a mild but curable illness.

"Recently relocated from the city. I needed somewhere quieter." He gestured vaguely toward the window, toward the fog that had taken up semi-permanent residence on Cormorant Street. "Somewhere with atmosphere."
"Blackwood Vale has atmosphere," Mrs. Thorne said dryly. "Whether it's the kind you want is another question."

Julian laughed — a real laugh, unhurried, as though he had nowhere else to be and no one else to be interesting for. "I've always found that the best places are the ones that aren't sure whether they want you."

He asked about the town's history then, and I should have been doing my work — there was a writing desk in the corner that needed its hinges oiled and its veneer stabilized — but I couldn't stop listening. He asked about the founding families. About the old properties on the hill. About the fog — how long it had been this thick, whether it was seasonal or something else. Each question was light, casual, the kind of thing a newcomer might ask. But there was a pattern to them, a through-line, as though he were assembling a map from scattered coordinates.
And his eyes kept drifting.
Not to Mrs. Thorne. Not to the watercolors she was pulling from storage. To me.

Every few seconds, his gaze would find its way back to my corner of the shop, touch me like a searchlight, and move on. I told myself it was curiosity. I told myself he was looking at the desk I was supposed to be working on. I told myself a lot of things.

Mrs. Thorne left to check the back room for a specific piece, and suddenly Julian and I were alone in the front of the shop with only the clock and a dozen dead people's belongings to keep us company.
He didn't waste the silence.
"What do you do with these objects?" he asked, nodding toward the writing desk. "You handle them like they're alive."

My hands stilled on the veneer. I hadn't realized I'd been touching the desk at all — my fingers had been moving in their default pattern, reading the surface the way braille readers read text. The grain. The joins. The tiny, almost invisible repairs made by whoever had loved this desk before.
"Antique restoration," I said. "Or that's the professional term. Mostly I just listen to them."
"Listen?"

"Every object carries its history. The marks. The wear patterns. You can tell where someone's hand rested every day for thirty years by the way the varnish thins. You can tell which drawers were used and which were secret. You can tell—"
I stopped. I was doing the thing I always do — the overexplaining, the enthusiasm that makes people's eyes glaze like day-old donuts. But Julian wasn't glazing. He was leaning closer.
"You see what other people miss," he said. "That's rare."

Four words. Eight syllables. The most dangerous thing anyone has ever said to me, though I didn't know it yet. What I knew, in that moment, was that someone had taken the thing I've spent my whole life feeling — the sense that I am perpetually observing a world that isn't observing me back — and reflected it. He'd seen the seeing. He'd named it.
And I felt the floor tilt, just slightly, in his direction.
"Most people look right through the past," I said, trying to sound casual and not quite managing it. "They want things clean and presentable. They don't want to know about the hands."

"I want to know about the hands." He was close enough now that I could smell the turpentine more clearly, and under it something warmer — cedar, maybe, or the mineral tang of oil paint. "That's why I paint. To show people what they've been standing next to their whole lives without noticing."
The shop bell rang.

The man who walked in was the opposite of an event. Flannel shirt. Work boots that had seen actual mud — crusted in it, in fact, along the soles and up to the ankle. He smelled like pine resin and cold air, and he walked to the counter with the purposeful efficiency of someone who knew exactly what he wanted and didn't require a conversation to get it.
"Trail maps," he said to me, since Mrs. Thorne was still in the back. "The updated ones, if you have them."

I found them in the drawer beneath the register. He paid in exact change — I remember that, because who carries exact change anymore? — and his fingers brushed mine for less than a second when he took the maps.
"Thanks," he said.
And he left.

I didn't notice his eyes. I didn't notice the way he paused at the door, just for a moment, as though he were going to say something else. I didn't notice the slight furrow in his brow when he looked at me — puzzled, like I was a question he hadn't studied for. I didn't notice any of it, because Julian Cross was still standing three feet away, and the room was still tilted, and my hands were still humming with someone else's warmth, and the forest ranger was just... wallpaper. Background. The kind of person you look through on your way to looking at something more compelling.
His eyes were blue, though. I noticed that later, the way you notice a sound only after it's gone.

Julian watched the door close behind the ranger — Leo, I'd later learn; Leo Hartwell — and something flickered across his face. Recognition, maybe. Or calculation. It was too fast to read, and then it was gone, replaced by that easy warmth he wore like a second skin.
"Your coworker?" he asked.
"Customer."
"Ah." He turned back to me. "Where were we?"
We were nowhere, I should have said. We were closing a transaction about provincial art that has nothing to do with me. But the words didn't come out in that order.
Instead, Julian asked if he could sketch me.
"Not paint," he said quickly, holding up his hands as though I'd already objected. "Just a sketch. Pencil on paper. I won't take much of your time."
He said it like time was the issue. Like the problem was scheduling. Like the only reason a woman might say no to a stranger who'd walked out of the fog was because her calendar was full.

I should have said no. I had reasons — dozens of them, lined up like the silverware in Mrs. Thorne's display case, each one polished and ready. I didn't know him. I didn't trust charm that came that easily. I didn't like the way the air felt when he was in the room, like something was pressing against the inside of my skin.
But he'd said I see what other people miss, and the words were still warm in my chest like tea I couldn't stop drinking.
"Maybe," I said.
He smiled.

Not a grin — nothing so obvious. Just the smallest relaxation around his eyes, the faint upward curve of someone who'd placed a bet and watched it land. He thanked me, left his number on a card that smelled faintly of linseed oil, and walked out into the fog.
The bell rang behind him. The shop felt emptier than it had before, though nothing had been removed.
Mrs. Thorne emerged from the back room holding a watercolor of the harbor that was, by any objective measure, terrible. She set it on the counter and looked at the door.
"That man arrived with the fog," she said.

It wasn't a metaphor. The fog had rolled in three days ago, thick and strange, and the town had been living inside it ever since. People talked about it the way they talk about weather — as though it were a condition of the atmosphere rather than a decision made by something with intentions.

I didn't ask Mrs. Thorne what she meant. I wasn't sure I wanted to know, and anyway, my hands were still humming, and my heart was still failing to sync with the clock, and somewhere under all of it, the floor was still tilted.

That night I sat at my desk with the blank notebook — the one without the lavender, without the pressed flowers, without my grandmother's careful centuries of warning. Just empty pages and the scratch of my pen.
*A man came with the fog. He said I see what others miss. He's wrong. I didn't see him coming.*

I underlined *coming* three times. Then I sat there looking at what I'd written, at the word made emphatic and then excessive and then almost desperate, three lines pressing into the page like I was trying to nail the meaning down before it could escape.
Then, smaller, in the margin:
*Grandma didn't leave her chair today. She hasn't looked at me since yesterday. I think she's afraid. Of what? Of him? Or of me?*

I closed the notebook. I could hear her through the wall — not moving, not speaking, just breathing. The steady, shallow respiration of someone who has decided that stillness is the only safe response to a threat they cannot name.

I wanted to go to her. I wanted to ask her what she knew about men who arrive with the fog, about gifts that hum under the skin, about the particular cruelty of being seen by someone you shouldn't want to see you back.

But I didn't. Because I was afraid of the answer. And because — this is the part I can barely admit — some part of me was still standing in that shop, still tilted toward the door, still holding the card that smelled like linseed oil and wondering what it would feel like to be sketched by someone who looked at you like you were a map he wanted to memorize.
The clock in the hall ticked. My heart stuttered. The fog pressed against the window, patient and plain, like someone waiting to be invited in.
I didn't invite it.
But I didn't close the curtains either.
